skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render videos from React/Remotion component code via inference.sh. Pass TSX code, get MP4. Supports all Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence. Configurable resolution, FPS, duration, codec. Use for: programmatic video generation, animated graphics, motion design, data-driven videos, React animations to video. Triggers: remotion, render video from code, tsx to video, react video, programmatic video, remotion render, code to video, animated video, motion graphics code, react animation video
skill.summary: Generate MP4 videos from React/Remotion TSX code using the belt CLI and inference.sh API.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 41 potential issues: 32 external commands, 6 hardcoded URLs, and 3 weak cryptographic patterns. All are false positives in this context. The file is a pure Markdown documentation file (SKILL.md) with no executable code. The 'external commands' are markdown code blocks showing belt CLI usage examples. The 'hardcoded URLs' are documentation links to inference.sh, remotion.dev, and GitHub. The 'weak crypto' findings are triggered by description text and table headers, not actual cryptographic code. The skill uses scoped tool permissions (Bash(belt *)) limiting execution to the belt CLI only. No malicious intent or prompt injection detected.

low_findings.title: External Command References in Documentation
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md file contains 32 references to belt CLI commands in markdown code blocks. These are documentation examples showing how to use the belt CLI to render videos, not actual command execution. The skill declares allowed-tools as 'Bash(belt *)' which restricts execution to the belt CLI only, providing appropriate scoping.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: The file contains 6 hardcoded URLs pointing to legitimate services: inference.sh (the video rendering platform), remotion.dev (official Remotion documentation), and GitHub (raw content and skill repositories). These are standard documentation references and pose no security risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Backtick Patterns in Markdown
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 32 locations as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are markdown code formatting backticks in documentation examples, not actual shell command execution. The file contains no Ruby code and no executable shell commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 3 locations for 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. Lines 3 and 35 are documentation text (the skill description and input schema table header). There is no cryptographic code in this file. The pattern is likely triggered by keyword matching in the description text.

content.user_title: Render Videos from React Code Instantly
content.value_statement: Converting React components into video files requires complex tooling and configuration. This skill lets you pass TSX code directly and get MP4 output without setup overhead.
actual_capabilities: Renders React/Remotion TSX components to MP4 video files via inference.sh
Supports all Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence, Audio, Video, Img
Configurable video parameters: resolution, FPS, duration, and codec
Provides both Bash CLI (belt) and Python SDK interfaces for video generation
Supports streaming progress updates for long-running render jobs
Accepts custom props passed to the root component for data-driven videos
limitations: Requires inference.sh account and belt CLI installation
Render time depends on inference.sh platform availability and queue
Limited to Remotion-compatible React patterns and supported APIs
Output format is MP4; other formats require post-processing

use_cases.title: Marketing Video Generation
use_cases.description: Create branded promotional videos from React component templates with dynamic props for personalized content at scale.

use_cases.title: Data Visualization Videos
use_cases.description: Generate animated charts and data-driven video reports by passing dynamic data as props to Remotion components.

use_cases.title: Social Media Content Automation
use_cases.description: Produce short-form animated content for social platforms using programmatic video generation from code.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Fade-In Animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a 3-second video at 30fps with a fade-in text animation saying 'Hello World' centered on a black background.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: creating a basic animated video

prompt_templates.title: Animated Counter
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 5-second 1080x1080 video at 60fps showing a loading percentage counter that animates from 0% to 100%.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: progress indicators and dynamic content

prompt_templates.title: Spring Physics Animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a 2-second 1080x1080 video with a white square that bounces in with spring physics (damping: 10, stiffness: 100) on an indigo background.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Sequence Story
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 4-second 1920x1080 video with three text elements ('First', 'Second', 'Third') that fade in sequentially using Remotion Sequence components.

output_examples.input: Render a 3-second fade-in title card saying 'Welcome' with dark background and white text.
output_examples.output: A 1920x1080 MP4 video showing 'Welcome' text fading from 0% to 100% opacity over 3 seconds at 30fps. Text is centered, white, 80pt font, on a dark navy (#1a1a2e) background.

output_examples.input: Generate a 5-second loading animation showing percentage progress from 0 to 100.
output_examples.output: A 1080x1080 MP4 at 60fps displaying a large percentage counter (200pt white text) that increments from 0% to 100% over 5 seconds, with 'Loading...' subtitle in gray below, on a black background.

output_examples.input: Create a spring-animated logo reveal with a 200x200 white rounded square on indigo background.
output_examples.output: A 1080x1080 MP4 at 60fps showing a white rounded square (20px border radius) scaling from 0 to full size with spring physics (damping: 10, stiffness: 100) over 2 seconds on an indigo (#6366f1) background.

best_practices: Keep component code self-contained with all imports at the top of the code string
Use useCurrentFrame and useVideoConfig for frame-accurate animations
Specify width, height, fps, and duration_seconds explicitly to avoid default assumptions
Test with short durations first (1-2 seconds) before rendering longer videos to validate component behavior
anti_patterns: Do not include external file references or network calls in the TSX code; use only Remotion and React imports
Do not use setTimeout or setInterval for animations; use Remotion's frame-based timing instead
Do not render extremely long videos (>60 seconds) without testing shorter durations first

faq.question: What is the belt CLI?
faq.answer: The belt CLI is the command-line interface for inference.sh, a platform for running AI and media generation apps. It handles authentication, app execution, and result retrieval.

faq.question: Do I need a Remotion installation locally?
faq.answer: No. The inference.sh platform runs the Remotion rendering server-side. You only need to provide valid TSX component code that uses supported Remotion APIs.

faq.question: Can I use custom React hooks in my video code?
faq.answer: Yes, as long as the hooks are defined within the same code string or imported from the remotion and react packages.

faq.question: How long does video rendering take?
faq.answer: Render time depends on video length, resolution, and platform load. Short clips (3-5 seconds) typically complete in under a minute. Longer videos may take several minutes.

faq.question: What output formats are supported?
faq.answer: The default output is MP4. The codec parameter allows you to specify different codecs, but MP4 is the standard container format.

faq.question: Can I stream the rendering progress?
faq.answer: Yes. The Python SDK supports a stream=True mode that yields progress updates as the video renders, allowing real-time monitoring of long jobs.
